concerns.text: The plan relies on a verified URL `nist_800_53` for corrosion data, which is explicitly identified in the text as a standard for 'security controls', not corrosion. The plan admits the schema will likely fail and proposes a 'Simulation Mode' fallback. A scientific validation of a predictive model for corrosion potential cannot be performed on synthetic data generated to match a schema; this renders the core research question (predicting real-world E_corr) untestable with the proposed data strategy.

The plan proposes merging 'NIST' (measurements) and 'Materials Project' (computed properties) via 'fuzzy match on alloy name'. Materials Project contains DFT-computed ground-state properties for idealized crystal structures, not experimental corrosion potentials in specific pH/temperature environments. There is no physical or empirical basis to assume a direct mapping exists between a static DFT entry and a dynamic electrochemical measurement. This creates a fundamental category error in the target variable definition.

The 'Leave-One-Alloy-Family-Out' (LOAO) split defines families by primary base metal (Fe, Ni, Al, Ti). If the dataset contains only a few families (e.g., only Fe and Ni), holding out one family leaves the test set with only one family. This results in a test set that is not a distribution of 'unseen alloy families' but a single-family extrapolation, which is statistically insufficient to validate generalization across 'diverse metallic systems' as claimed in the spec.

The plan uses 'One-sample permutation test' with only 100 permutations to generate p-values for feature importance. For a rigorous scientific claim of significance (p < 0.05) with Bonferroni correction, 100 permutations provide a minimum p-value resolution of 0.01, which is too coarse for robust statistical inference, especially with multiple comparisons. Standard practice requires >1,000 permutations for stable p-value estimation in this context.
